description: King of Osroene (177–212); likely the first Christian monarch; tradition holds he converted after contact with early Christian missionaries; protected Christians in his realm.
Bishop of Caesarea Maritima (340–366); leading Arian/homoian theologian; pupil of Eusebius of Caesarea; key figure at councils of Antioch (341) and Seleucia (359).
Named in 1 Corinthians among those who visited Paul.
Bishop of Alexandria (311–312); successor of Peter of Alexandria; ordained Arius as deacon.

apostolic_connection: Missionary to Edessa credited with founding Syriac Christianity.
description: Syriac apostle whose mission to Edessa is foundational for the Church of the East; the Doctrine of Addai recounts the mission.
citations: Doctrine of Addai; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 1.13
notes: Possibly same as Thaddaeus; apostolic legend of Edessa.

description: Bishop present at the Council of Arles (314); likely represented Colchester or Lincoln in Roman Britain.
Companion of Frumentius captured by Axumite king Ella Amida; became a trusted court official and later returned to Tyre; helped lay groundwork for Ethiopian Christianity.
Martyr of Augsburg executed under Diocletian c.304; venerated as patron saint of Augsburg; Acts of her martyrdom survive.
Prophet who foretold a famine and later warned Paul.
Second bishop of Edessa; disciple of Addai according to the Doctrine of Addai; traditionally martyred by Abgar the Black.
Early bishop of Trier; present at the Council of Arles (314); venerated locally as a saint.
Early bishop of Alexandria (c.167-188) in the Coptic succession list.
First recorded Christian martyr in Britain; sheltered a priest and converted; executed under Diocletian c.304.
Simon of Cyrene's son mentioned in Mark 15:21; possibly known to the Roman Christian community.
Bishop of Rome (c.107-115); associated with early Roman liturgical tradition.
Bishop of Alexandria (312-328) who first condemned Arius and championed the Nicene position; mentor to Athanasius.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 10.5; Theodoret Ecclesiastical History 1

description: Bishop of Byzantium who preceded Metrophanes; died c.314.
Bishop of Constantinople (314–337); succeeded Metrophanes; strong supporter of Nicene orthodoxy; opposed Arianism throughout his episcopate.
Bishop of Jerusalem (c.212–251); co-bishop with Narcissus; founded the Jerusalem theological library; died in prison under Decius.
Bishop of Milan (374-397); converted from catechumen to bishop in one week; major opponent of Arianism in the West; influenced Augustine.
citations: Augustine Confessions; Paulinus Life of Ambrose
notes: Refused to surrender Milan churches to Empress Justina.

description: Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.

apostolic_connection: Early Roman bishop in traditional succession lists.
description: Figure in early lists of bishops of Rome; chronology and details are uncertain.
notes: Dates and historicity details are debated in scholarship.

description: Second bishop of Alexandria (c.68-83) according to Coptic tradition.
Disciple in Damascus who restored Paul's sight and baptized him.
Apostle and early disciple of Jesus.
Named by Paul among his kin and fellow prisoners; noted in Rom 16:7.
Bishop of Rome (c.155-166); hosted Polycarp of Smyrna who visited Rome; involved in early Easter dating dispute.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 4.19; Irenaeus in Eusebius

description: Prophetess in Luke who speaks about the child Jesus.
Bishop of Nicomedia martyred at the start of the Diocletianic Great Persecution (303).
Father of Christian monasticism; withdrew to the Egyptian desert c.270; his Life by Athanasius became foundational for monasticism.
citations: Athanasius Life of Anthony; Jerome Lives of the Fathers
notes: Feast day 17 January; died at age c.105.

description: Early martyr at Pergamon mentioned in Revelation 2:13 as a faithful witness killed in the city where Satan dwells.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Bishop of Hierapolis (c.175); early apologist who addressed works to Marcus Aurelius; wrote against Montanism; cited by Eusebius.
Bishop of Hierapolis (c.170-180); wrote apologies to Marcus Aurelius and works against Montanism.

apostolic_connection: Alexandrian teacher instructed by Priscilla and Aquila; associated with Corinth (Acts 18).
description: Alexandrian Jewish Christian teacher influential in early churches.
Named among recipients of Philemon.

apostolic_connection: Coworker of Paul with Priscilla; hosted house-churches.
description: Jewish Christian coworker of Paul, linked with Priscilla in Acts and letters.
Named in Philemon and Colossians; exhorted to fulfill ministry.
First catholicos of Armenia after Gregory the Illuminator; his father's successor; attended Council of Nicaea (325); martyred c.333.

apostolic_connection: Travel companion mentioned in Acts and letters.
description: Macedonian travel companion of Paul, mentioned in Acts and Pauline letters.
Athenian Christian apologist; presented an apology defending Christians to Emperor Hadrian (or Antoninus Pius) c.124-140; earliest surviving apology genre contrasting Christians with pagans and Jews.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 4.3; Jerome De Viris Illustribus 20

description: Named in Romans 16 via greetings to his household.
Libyan-born priest in Alexandria whose teaching that the Son was a creature not co-eternal with the Father sparked the Arian controversy and the Council of Nicaea.
citations: Athanasius Orations Against the Arians; Council of Nicaea 325
notes: Died suddenly in Constantinople 336, the day before his planned restoration.

description: Bishop of Antioch (c.211–220); confessor who suffered under Septimius Severus; praised by Origen in a letter.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Bishop of Alexandria (328-373) who championed Nicene homoousios against Arianism; exiled five times; authored Life of Anthony and Easter Festal Letters.
notes: Athanasius contra mundum; Easter Festal Letter 367 first lists the full NT canon.

description: Christian philosopher and apologist; addressed Plea for the Christians (Legatio) to Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Commodus c.177; also wrote On the Resurrection of the Dead.
Bishop of Milan (355–374); Arian; installed by Constantius II over Western objections; opposed by Hilary of Poitiers; predecessor of Ambrose.
Bishop of Alexandria (c.84–98); second bishop after Annianus in the Alexandrian episcopal list.
Early bishop of the Church of the East; listed in the traditional patriarchal succession of Seleucia-Ctesiphon.
Bishop of Antioch (c.237–253); reportedly refused Emperor Philip the Arab entry to church until he did penance; died in prison under Decius; his relics later moved by Gallus.
citations: Eusebius, Eccl. Hist. 6.29; Chrysostom, De S. Babyla

description: Bishop of Corinth; correspondent of Dionysius of Corinth (c.170).
Catholicos of the Church of the East (342–346); succeeded Shahdost; martyred under Shapur II during the Great Persecution.
Syriac philosopher and hymnographer; wrote Book of the Laws of Countries; cosmological views later judged heterodox.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 4.30; Book of the Laws of Countries
notes: Father of Syriac hymnody; Ephrem later wrote against his views.

apostolic_connection: Jerusalem disciple; missionary companion of Paul (Acts).
description: Early missionary leader who partnered with Paul and helped integrate converts.
Apostle listed among the Twelve; later traditions vary.
Bishop of Ancyra (c.336–360); leader of the homoiousian party; opposed both strict Arianism and Nicene homoousios; deposed multiple times.

Bishop of Caesarea in Cappadocia (370–379); one of the Cappadocian Fathers; formulated the theology of the Holy Spirit; founded communal monasticism in the East; author of the Longer and Shorter Rules.
citations: De Spiritu Sancto; Basilian Rule; Letters

description: Gnostic teacher in Alexandria (c.117-138); founder of the Basilidean school; taught 365 heavens and salvation through gnosis.
Bishop of Bostra (c.220–240); taught that Christ had no pre-existent deity; corrected by Origen at a synod c.240.
Christian slave martyred in the persecution of Lyon and Vienne (177); her endurance became legendary in the early church.
citations: Letter of the Churches of Lyon and Vienne in Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 5.1

description: Bishop of Carthage (c.312-340) whose contested consecration triggered the Donatist schism.
citations: Augustine Against the Donatists; Optatus Against the Donatists
notes: Council of Arles (314) upheld his consecration.

description: Pope (283–296); traditionally attributed authorship of a Dialogue against Proclus the Montanist; venerated as a martyr though evidence is late.
Bishop of Rome (c.217-222); formerly a slave; admitted remarried clergy and restored the lapsed; opposed by Hippolytus of Rome.
citations: Hippolytus Refutation of All Heresies 9; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 6
notes: Built the Catacombs of Callixtus on Via Appia.

description: Bishop of Jericho; attended the Council of Nicaea (325) according to some episcopal lists.
Named in 2 Timothy as holding Paul's cloak and books at Troas.
Gnostic teacher of the late 2nd century; associated with Docetism and severe asceticism; founder of an encratite sect; cited by Clement of Alexandria.
Bishop of Alexandria (c.152–166); in the apostolic succession list of Eusebius.
Gnostic teacher in Rome (c.137–140); taught a dualist theology distinguishing the God of the Old Testament from the God of Jesus; teacher of Marcion.
citations: Irenaeus, Against Heresies 1.27.1; Eusebius, Eccl. Hist. 4.11

description: Named in 2 Timothy among those sending greetings.
Head of the Alexandrian catechetical school; synthesized Christian faith with Greek philosophy; wrote Protrepticus, Paedagogus, and Stromata.
notes: Teacher of Origen; fled Alexandria during the Severan persecution c.202.

apostolic_connection: Linked with the Roman church in the late 1st century; associated with 1 Clement.
description: Late-1st-century Roman church leader traditionally associated with the letter known as 1 Clement.
notes: Date and office terminology debated; often treated as an Apostolic Father.

description: Named in Philippians among Paul's coworkers whose names are in the book of life.
Disciple in the Emmaus road narrative in Luke 24.
Roman Emperor (306-337); issued Edict of Milan (313) granting religious tolerance; convened Council of Nicaea (325); baptized on his deathbed.
notes: First Christian Roman emperor; founded Constantinople.

description: Roman Caesar and Augustus; father of Constantine I; relatively tolerant of Christians in his western territories during the Diocletianic persecution.
citations: Eusebius Life of Constantine; Lactantius De Mortibus Persecutorum

apostolic_connection: First major Gentile convert in Acts 10.
description: Roman centurion whose household receives baptism in Acts 10.
Named in 2 Timothy as having gone to Galatia.

apostolic_connection: Convert in Corinth during Paul's mission (Acts 18:8).
description: Ruler of the synagogue in Corinth who believed with his household.
Bishop of Carthage (249-258); theologian of church unity; executed under Valerian; author of De Unitate Ecclesiae.
Bishop of Jerusalem (350-386); author of the Catechetical Lectures, a primary source for 4th-century baptismal instruction and liturgy; embroiled in Arian controversies and deposed three times.
notes: Doctor of the Church; feast day 18 March.

description: Athenian woman convert named in Acts 17:34.
Pauline coworker mentioned in Colossians and Philemon; later noted as having deserted in 2 Timothy.
Bishop of Antioch (c.253–260); succeeded Fabius; led church during Valerian persecution.
Bishop of Alexandria (c.189–232); organized the catechetical school; appointed Origen as its head, later excommunicated him; first bishop to develop a broad episcopal network in Egypt.
Athenian convert named in Acts 17:34.
Bishop of Alexandria (248-264); student of Origen; navigated the Decian and Valerian persecutions; wrote on Easter dating and authorship of Revelation.
notes: Called the Great by the Eastern church.

description: Bishop of Corinth (c.170); wrote pastoral letters to multiple churches including Rome, Athens, Sparta, and Gortyna, preserved in fragments by Eusebius; emphasized church unity and charitable support.
Bishop of Rome (260-268); reorganized the Roman church after the Valerian persecution; corresponded with Dionysius of Alexandria on Trinitarian theology.
Bishop of Alexandria (c.232–247); succeeded Heraclas in the Alexandrian episcopal succession.
Bishop of Antioch (c.268–272); succeeded Paul of Samosata after his condemnation; inherited the church buildings after imperial intervention.
Leader of the Donatist schism who gave his name to the movement; Bishop of Carthage in the Donatist succession; opposed Caecilian for over four decades.
citations: Augustine Against the Donatists; Optatus Against the Donatists

description: Bishop of Casae Nigrae in Numidia; founder of the Donatist movement; argued that Caecilian's ordination was invalid due to his ordainer Felix being a traditor; his followers became the Donatists.
citations: Optatus of Mileve, Against the Donatists

description: Bishop of York; attended the Council of Arles (314); one of the earliest attested British bishops.
Pope (c.174–189); during his pontificate Irenaeus of Lyon visited Rome; tradition records correspondence with the British king Lucius.
Named in Romans 16 as among Paul's acquaintances in Rome.
Coworker associated with Colossae; commended in Colossians.
Messenger and coworker from Philippi associated with Paul's imprisonment.
Syriac deacon and hymnographer; fled to Edessa when Nisibis was ceded to Persia (363); wrote against Arianism, Marcionism, and Bardaisanism; Doctor of the Church.
notes: Called the Harp of the Holy Spirit; feast day 9 June.

description: Bishop of Salamis in Cyprus (367–403); author of the Panarion (Medicine Chest), an encyclopedic refutation of 80 heresies; staunch defender of Nicene orthodoxy; opponent of Origen's legacy.

apostolic_connection: Named in Rom 16:23; also in Acts/letters.
description: Named as city treasurer in Romans 16:23; appears elsewhere in the Pauline circle.
Bishop of Alexandria (c.189) in the episcopal list; little else is recorded.
Named in 2 Timothy among those sending greetings.
Traditionally regarded as the first bishop of Trier; sent from Rome in the 1st or 2nd century according to local tradition; historical attestation is weak.
Bishop in the Alexandrian succession; minimal information survives.
Bishop of Alexandria (c.129–141); in the apostolic succession list preserved by Eusebius.
Mother of Timothy (2 Tim 1:5); Jewish believer who together with her mother Lois raised Timothy in the scriptures; likely converted early in Paul's mission to Lystra.
Named in Philippians among two women urged to agree in the Lord.
Bishop of Caesarea; author of the Ecclesiastical History, foundational source for early Christian history; adviser to Constantine I.
notes: His Ecclesiastical History covers c.33-324 CE.

description: Arian bishop of Nicomedia (then Beirut and Constantinople); ally of Arius; baptized Constantine I on his deathbed (337); influential at the Arian-favoring imperial court.
citations: Socrates Scholasticus Ecclesiastical History 2; Athanasius Historia Arianorum

description: Bishop of Antioch (c.324–327); leading anti-Arian theologian; deposed at the Council of Antioch (327/328) on charges of Sabellianism; his writings include fragments against Origen.
Bishop of Sebaste (c.357-377); early monastic pioneer in Pontus and Armenia; influenced Basil of Caesarea; condemned at the Council of Gangra (c.340) for extreme asceticism.
citations: Basil of Caesarea letters; Council of Gangra canons

description: Pope (275–283); little is known; traditional martyrdom account is late and unreliable.
Young man in Acts 20 who fell from a window and was restored to life.

apostolic_connection: Early Roman bishop in traditional succession lists (late 1st/early 2nd c).
description: Early bishop of Rome in later episcopal lists; details uncertain.
notes: Often dated late 1st century into early 2nd.

description: Traditional first bishop of Antioch after Peter; said to have coined the term \\\"Christian\\\" (Acts 11:26) or to have introduced it in Antioch; succeeded by Ignatius.
Bishop of Rome (236-250); divided Roman church into 7 deaconries; martyred in the Decian persecution (250); Origen wrote a letter to him about his own teachings.
Bishop of Antioch (c.250–253); corresponded with Cyprian and Cornelius of Rome; initially sympathetic to Novatian's rigorist position.
Enslaved woman martyred with Perpetua in Carthage (7 March 203); gave birth in prison days before her execution; account preserved in the Passio Perpetuae.

Jewish-Christian chronicler and church historian; wrote five books of Memoirs (Hypomnemata, c.170–180) tracing apostolic succession in Rome, Corinth, and Jerusalem to demonstrate the unity and continuity of apostolic teaching against Gnostic claims; visited Rome under Anicetus and stayed until Eleutherius; preserved the earliest known list of Roman bishops; a primary source for Eusebius's Ecclesiastical History.
Pope (269–274); little historical documentation; traditional account credits him with regulating mass on martyrs' tombs.

Bishop of Aptunga; ordained Caecilian as Bishop of Carthage (311); accused by Donatists of being a traditor who surrendered scriptures under Diocletian; a formal inquiry by the proconsul Aelianus found him innocent.
citations: Optatus of Mileve, Against the Donatists 1.14

description: Bishop of Caesarea Cappadocia (c.232-268); student of Origen; ally of Cyprian in the baptism controversy; participated in Paul of Samosata controversy.
Bishop of Antioch (c.333–341); Arian sympathizer; presided over the synod that condemned Athanasius of Alexandria (c.341).
Bishop of Aquileia (c.343–360); noted for biblical commentaries; pressured Pope Liberius to condemn Athanasius; his Commentary on the Gospels was rediscovered in 2012.
Named in 1 Corinthians among those who visited Paul.
Bishop of Tarragona; martyred with his deacons Augurius and Eulogius under Valerian (21 January 259); account preserved in Acts of Fructuosus; Prudentius wrote a hymn in his honor.
Tyrian Christian who became tutor to the Aksumite royal court; consecrated by Athanasius as first bishop of Ethiopia (c.328); evangelized King Ezana.
notes: Called Abba Selama (Father of Peace) in Ethiopia.

description: Bishop of Alexandria (c.260); in the Alexandrian episcopal succession list; minimal information survives.

apostolic_connection: Host mentioned in Rom 16:23; also appears in other NT contexts.
description: Named as host of Paul and the whole church in Romans 16:23.
Companion of Paul on his final journey to Jerusalem (Acts 20:4); from Derbe in Galatia.
Bishop of Caesarea Maritima (c.367–395); nephew of Cyril of Jerusalem; continued Eusebius's Ecclesiastical History; works largely lost.
Bishop of Carthage (c.349); presided over the Council of Carthage (349) that declared Donatist re-baptism invalid.
citations: Optatus of Mileve, Against the Donatists

description: Cappadocian Father and theologian; briefly Bishop of Constantinople (380-381); presided at the Council of Constantinople (381); his Five Theological Orations are foundational Trinitarian texts.
notes: One of the Three Holy Hierarchs.

description: Bishop of Neocaesarea (c.240-270); student of Origen; credited with miraculous healings; the church he built reportedly grew from 17 to all-but-17-pagans during his tenure.
citations: Gregory of Nyssa Life of Gregory Thaumaturgus; Jerome

description: Bishop of Nazianzus (c.328-374); father of Gregory Nazianzus the Theologian; converted from the Hypsistarian sect.
Apostle of Armenia; converted King Tiridates III (c.301); became first Catholicos of the Armenian Apostolic Church; Armenia is credited as the first nation to adopt Christianity.
notes: Imprisoned for 13 years in a pit before converting the king.

description: Mother of Emperor Constantine I; converted to Christianity; made a pilgrimage to the Holy Land c.326-328 where she founded churches and reportedly discovered the True Cross.
citations: Eusebius Life of Constantine 3.41-47; Socrates Scholasticus

description: Bishop of Alexandria (232–248); pupil and successor of Origen as head of the catechetical school; succeeded Demetrius as bishop; first Alexandrian bishop to wear the episcopal miter.
Early bishop of Cyprus; traditionally connected to the apostolic mission of Paul and Barnabas.
Traditional first bishop of Aquileia; said to have been sent by Mark the Evangelist; historical documentation is medieval.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Bishop of Alexandria (c.179–189) in the episcopal succession list preserved by Eusebius.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Bishop of Alexandria (c.189) in the Eusebian episcopal list; minimal information survives.
Roman theologian and antipope; wrote Refutation of All Heresies and Apostolic Tradition; opponent of popes Callixtus I and Zephyrinus; exiled to Sardinian mines with Pope Pontian where he died.
notes: The Apostolic Tradition attributed to him is a key source for early liturgy.

description: Bishop of Cordoba (c.295-357); principal religious adviser to Constantine I; presided at Nicaea (325); possibly the main drafter of the Nicene Creed.
citations: Eusebius Life of Constantine; Athanasius Historia Arianorum
notes: Signed an Arian formula under pressure at Council of Sirmium (357) aged over 100.

description: Catholicos of Armenia (c.340–347); grandson of Gregory the Illuminator; martyred by King Tiran of Armenia.
Pope (c.136–142); listed in early papal succession lists; Irenaeus records him as a contemporary of Valentinus and Cerdo.
citations: Irenaeus, Against Heresies 3.4.3; Eusebius, Eccl. Hist. 4.10

description: False teacher condemned by Paul (1 Tim 1:20; 2 Tim 2:17); taught that the resurrection had already occurred; excommunicated.

apostolic_connection: Disciple of John the Apostle in later tradition.
description: Bishop of Antioch and early Church Father; wrote seven letters en route to martyrdom in Rome; key witness to early episcopacy and Eucharistic theology.
notes: Letters are primary sources for mono-episcopal church order in the 2nd century.

apostolic_connection: Student of Polycarp of Smyrna who had known the Apostle John.
description: Bishop of Lyon (c.178-202); author of Against Heresies; formulated the apostolic succession and rule of faith arguments against Gnosticism; bridge between the apostolic and post-apostolic generations.
notes: His Against Heresies is the most important anti-gnostic work of the 2nd century.

description: Bishop of Sirmium; martyred under Diocletian (6 April 304); Acts of his martyrdom survive and record his interrogation before the governor Probus.
Bishop of Nisibis (c.308-338); attended the Council of Nicaea (325); credited with miraculous defense of Nisibis against Persian sieges; teacher of Ephrem the Syrian.
citations: Theodoret Ecclesiastical History 1.11; Ephrem Hymns on Nisibis

description: Apostle listed among the Twelve.

apostolic_connection: One of the Twelve; executed in Jerusalem (Acts 12:2).
description: Apostle in Jesus' inner circle; first of the Twelve reported martyred in Acts.
notes: Acts 12:2 is the primary textual anchor for his death.

name_alt: James of Jerusalem;brother of the Lord
apostolic_connection: Named by Paul as a Jerusalem 'pillar' alongside Peter and John (Gal 2).
description: Leader of the Jerusalem church in the mid-1st century; central to early Gentile inclusion debates.
notes: Death date is approximate; later sources differ on circumstances.

description: Bishop of Benevento; martyred under Diocletian (c.305); patron saint of Naples; known for the relic of his liquefying blood preserved at Naples Cathedral.

apostolic_connection: Associated with Thessalonica (Acts 17) and named in Rom 16:21.
description: Host of Paul and companions in Thessalonica; named among Paul's associates.
1st-century Jewish teacher whose crucifixion and claimed resurrection are foundational to Christianity.
notes: Public ministry c. 27–30; crucifixion under Pontius Pilate (common scholarly dating).

apostolic_connection: Named among women who supported Jesus (Luke 8:3).
description: Disciple named in Luke among women who supported Jesus' ministry.
Bishop of Jerusalem; in the Jerusalem episcopal succession; distinct from Cyril of Jerusalem's successor John II (387–417) but possibly the same person in some lists.

name_alt: John the Divine; John the Revelator
description: Christian prophet exiled to the island of Patmos; author of the Book of Revelation (Apocalypse of John); traditionally identified with John the Apostle, though scholarly opinion on authorship is divided.
citations: Revelation 1:1,9; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 3.18; Justin Martyr Dialogue with Trypho 81

apostolic_connection: One of the Twelve; associated with Johannine tradition and later Asia Minor leadership.
description: Apostle traditionally linked to the Fourth Gospel and Johannine letters; later tradition places him at Ephesus.
notes: Death traditionally late 1st century; details disputed.

apostolic_connection: Prepared for Jesus' ministry; baptized Jesus.
description: Jewish preacher and baptizer central to the origins of Jesus' movement; executed by Herod Antipas.
notes: Execution traditionally before Jesus' death (c. late 20s/early 30s).

description: Husband of Mary and foster father of Jesus; a carpenter (Matt 13:55); of the Davidic line (Matt 1:16); appears in the infancy narratives and the Temple visit (Luke 2); absent from Jesus's public ministry, presumed deceased.
Candidate proposed to replace Judas Iscariot before Matthias was chosen.

apostolic_connection: Connected to Jesus' early life traditions.
description: According to the Gospels, husband of Mary and guardian of Jesus in infancy narratives.

apostolic_connection: Provided tomb for Jesus' burial in Gospel narratives.
description: Figure in passion narratives who secures Jesus' burial.
Jewish Christian count (comes) granted privileges by Constantine to build churches in Jewish cities of Galilee; described by Epiphanius.

apostolic_connection: Jerusalem leader sent with Silas to Antioch (Acts 15:22).
description: Jerusalem leader and prophet sent with the council letter to Antioch.

apostolic_connection: One of the Twelve; betrayer of Jesus.
description: Apostle known for betraying Jesus in the passion narratives.
Apostle listed among the Twelve; associated with the Epistle of Jude in tradition.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Roman emperor (361–363); nephew of Constantine I; raised Christian but renounced Christianity; attempted to revive paganism and curtailed Christian privileges; killed on campaign against Persia.
Pope (337–352); strong defender of Nicene orthodoxy; championed Athanasius and Marcellus of Ancyra against their Eastern accusers; presided over Council of Rome (340).
citations: Athanasius, Apologia contra Arianos; Eusebius, Eccl. Hist.

description: Named by Paul among his kin and fellow prisoners; noted in Rom 16:7.
Samaritan-born philosopher who converted to Christianity; wrote two Apologies and Dialogue with Trypho; described early Christian worship; executed in Rome under Marcus Aurelius.
notes: His Apologies are the most detailed accounts of early 2nd-century Christian worship and theology.

description: Bishop of Jerusalem (c.107–113) in the Eusebian list; minimal historical information.
Bishop of Alexandria in the succession list; mentioned by Eusebius.

King of Osroene (c.4 BC–7 AD and 13–50 AD); subject of the legendary correspondence with Jesus preserved in Eusebius; the letters are considered apocryphal by modern scholars but were influential in Eastern Christian tradition.
Aksumite king (c.320-360) who converted to Christianity through Frumentius; the first Christian king of Ethiopia; his coins changed from pagan to Christian symbols after conversion.
citations: Rufinus Ecclesiastical History 10.9; Ezana Stone inscriptions
notes: Inscription stones document his conversion and military campaigns.

description: King of Kartli (Iberia) who converted to Christianity c.327 after a miraculous healing attributed to Saint Nino; brought Christianity to Georgia.
notes: His conversion established Christianity as the state religion of Georgia.

description: King of Armenia (c.287-330) who was converted to Christianity by Gregory the Illuminator c.301; Armenia became the first kingdom to adopt Christianity as state religion.
citations: Agathangelos History of Armenia; Faustus of Byzantium
notes: His conversion traditionally dated to 301 CE.

description: Syrian Christian merchant who led a group of Christian immigrants to Kerala (India) c.345; regarded as a founder of the Saint Thomas Christian community (Knanaya) in India.

apostolic_connection: Associated with Jesus' ministry in Bethany (John 11).
description: Figure in John whose raising is central to the narrative.
Bishop of Antioch (344–358); homoian Arian; permitted Arian hymns; managed the Arian-dominated Eastern church after the Council of Antioch (341).

apostolic_connection: One of the Twelve; traditionally linked with the Gospel of Matthew.
description: Apostle traditionally associated with the Gospel of Matthew.

Pope (352–366); exiled by Constantius II for refusing to condemn Athanasius; under pressure signed an Arian-leaning formula in exile; returned to Rome 358; his signature on the formula remains historically controversial.

apostolic_connection: Mentioned in 2 Tim 4:21; later tradition connects him with Rome.
description: Named in 2 Timothy; later tradition lists Linus among early Roman bishops.
Grandmother of Timothy (2 Tim 1:5); praised by Paul for sincere faith; likely a Jewish believer.
Antiochene theologian and biblical scholar; founded influential school of biblical interpretation; teacher of Arius and Eusebius of Nicomedia; martyred under Maximinus Daia.
notes: His Arian-leaning theology influenced his students who formed the Arian party at Nicaea.

description: Pope (252–253); exiled by Trebonianus Gallus; returned before his death; Cyprian corresponded with him.
Named among prophets and teachers in the Antioch church (Acts 13:1).

apostolic_connection: Close coworker of Paul in tradition; attributed author of Luke–Acts.
description: Traditionally attributed author of the Gospel of Luke and Acts of the Apostles.

apostolic_connection: First convert in Philippi in Acts 16.
description: Merchant in Philippi whose household was baptized; hosted Paul and companions.
Bishop of Jerusalem (c.312–335); oversaw construction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre under Constantine; present at Council of Nicaea (325) as a leading Nicene bishop.
Bishop of Constantinople (c.342–360); semi-Arian; condemned the full divinity of the Holy Spirit; the Macedonian heresy (Pneumatomachi) is named after him.
Named among prophets and teachers in the Antioch church (Acts 13:1).
Pope (296–304); allegedly apostatized under Diocletian by offering incense to Roman gods; later tradition rehabilitated him; died 304 possibly by execution.
Pope (308–309); his brief pontificate was disrupted by Donatist-like controversy over lapsi; possibly died in servitude; commemorated as a martyr.
Bishop of Ancyra (314–374); anti-Arian; accused of Sabellianism for his Trinitarian theology; deposed multiple times; close ally of Athanasius; his theology influenced the Nicene Creed.
Bishop-centurion of Merida who renounced military service on a feast day (298); martyred under Maximian; his acts are well-preserved.

Shipowner and theologian from Sinope; moved to Rome c.140; excommunicated 144; taught that the OT God was a lesser demiurge distinct from the loving God of Jesus; founded extensive Marcionite network.
citations: Tertullian Against Marcion; Irenaeus Against Heresies 1.27
notes: His canon formation drove proto-orthodox NT canon development.

description: Bishop of Arles who adopted Novatianism; deposed c.254; Cyprian of Carthage wrote to the Gallic bishops urging his removal.
Pope (January–October 336); very brief pontificate; traditionally founded two basilicas in Rome.
Soldier martyr of Caesarea Maritima (c.262); about to be promoted to centurion but denounced as a Christian; beheaded; story preserved by Eusebius.

apostolic_connection: Associated with Peter in early tradition; coworker of Paul/Barnabas in Acts.
description: Traditionally linked to the Gospel of Mark and early mission activity.

apostolic_connection: Associated with Jesus' ministry in Bethany.
description: Sister of Mary and Lazarus in the Gospels.

name_alt: Mary, Mother of Jesus; Blessed Virgin Mary; Theotokos
description: Virgin mother of Jesus of Nazareth; wife of Joseph; central figure in the infancy narratives; present at the crucifixion (John 19:25) and in the upper room (Acts 1:14); venerated in Catholic and Orthodox tradition as Theotokos.

apostolic_connection: Witness to crucifixion and resurrection in Gospel narratives.
description: Disciple and prominent resurrection witness in the Gospels.

apostolic_connection: Associated with Jesus' ministry in Bethany.
description: Sister of Martha and Lazarus in the Gospel of John.
Named in Romans 16 as someone who worked hard for the community.
Bishop of Cologne (and possibly Trier simultaneously); attended the Council of Rome (313) and the Council of Arles (314); one of the earliest attested bishops of Cologne.
Apostle selected after the ascension to replace Judas Iscariot.

Montanist prophetess; with Montanus and Priscilla, one of the three founding figures of the New Prophecy movement; claimed to be the last prophet before the end; her prophecies were recorded and circulated.

Roman emperor (310–313); nephew of Galerius; fiercely persecuted Christians in the East even after the Edict of Serdica (311); defeated by Licinius at the Battle of Tzirallum (313); died shortly after.
Bishop of Alexandria (c.264–282); received Paul of Samosata's condemnation; corresponded with Dionysius of Rome.
Bishop of Jerusalem (c.333–348); confessor who lost an eye under Maximinus Daia; succeeded Macarius; later ordained Cyril of Jerusalem.
Bishop of Jerusalem (c.251–260); succeeded Alexander of Jerusalem; minimal records survive.
Bishop of Lycopolis in Egypt; founder of the Meletian Schism; clashed with Peter of Alexandria over re-admission of the lapsed during the Diocletianic persecution.
citations: Epiphanius Panarion 68; Athanasius Apologia Contra Arianos

description: Bishop of Sardis (c.150-180); apologist; produced the first known list of OT books; wrote a Paschal homily (On Pascha) that is an early example of anti-Jewish rhetoric and high Christology.
notes: On Pascha is an extraordinary example of early Christian liturgical rhetoric.

description: Bishop of Carthage (c.303–311); refused to surrender scriptures during Diocletian's persecution while hiding them; his death triggered the Caecilianist-Donatist schism.
citations: Optatus of Mileve, Against the Donatists 1.16

description: Bishop of Milan; attended the Council of Arles (314); one of the earliest attested bishops of Milan.
Philosopher-merchant of Tyre who transported Frumentius and Aedesius to Ethiopia c.316; killed when their ship was seized; his journey inadvertently initiated the Christianization of Ethiopia.
First bishop of Byzantium to be elevated to the status of archbishop; contemporary of Constantine; his successor Alexander became the first Bishop of Constantinople.
Pope (311–314); pontificate coincided with the Edict of Milan (313); presided over the Synod of Rome (313) that condemned Donatism.
Early disciple who hosted Paul and companions on arrival near Jerusalem.
Phrygian prophet who claimed to be the mouthpiece of the Paraclete; founded the New Prophecy (Montanist) movement c.156-172; taught imminent eschatology and strict asceticism.
Bishop of Jerusalem (c.185–211); known for miracles (water into oil); falsely accused and went into hiding; returned to office; co-bishop with Alexander of Jerusalem in old age.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named among the Seven in Acts 6.
Bishop of Myra in Lycia (c.317-343); venerated for charitable acts; traditionally attended Nicaea (325); origin of the Santa Claus legend.
notes: Feast day 6 December; relics translated to Bari 1087.

apostolic_connection: Associated with Jesus in John's Gospel.
description: Pharisee who appears in the Gospel of John; associated with Jesus' burial narrative.
Named among the Seven in Acts 6 as a proselyte from Antioch.
Cappadocian Christian woman who evangelized the Kingdom of Iberia (Georgia) c.327; her preaching converted Queen Nana and King Mirian III; regarded as Equal to the Apostles in Orthodox tradition.
Theologian and anti-pope of Rome (251); rigorist who opposed readmission of the lapsi; founded the Novatianist schism; first Latin theologian to write on the Trinity; martyred under Valerian.
House church host greeted by Paul (Col 4:15); a woman in Laodicea or Colossae who hosted a Christian assembly.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Figure in Philemon; named in Colossians as returning with Tychicus.
Commended in 2 Timothy for refreshing Paul and searching for him in Rome.
Greatest early Christian biblical scholar; wrote Hexapla, Contra Celsum, De Principiis; pupil of Clement; suffered under Decius; later condemned posthumously.
notes: Condemned at 2nd Council of Constantinople (553) for speculative theology.

description: Egyptian monk who founded the first cenobitic (communal) monastic movement c.320 at Tabennisi; established the Rule of Pachomius; his sister Mary founded the first women's monastery; by his death the Pachomian federation had thousands of monks.
Bishop of Edessa (c.180–200); ordained by Serapion of Antioch; his orthodox succession was contested by the Bardesanites; Palutians = orthodox Edessan Christians.
citations: Doctrine of Addai; Eusebius, Eccl. Hist. 1.13

description: Devoted disciple and biographer of Origen; founded library at Caesarea; teacher of Eusebius; martyred in the Diocletianic persecution.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 7.32; Life of Pamphilus
notes: Eusebius adopted his name (Eusebius Pamphili) in honor.

description: Head of the Alexandrian catechetical school (c.180–200); teacher of Clement of Alexandria; tradition records a missionary journey to India; first known head of the Alexandrian school.
First catholicos of the Church of the East to assert primacy of Seleucia-Ctesiphon; reorganized the Mesopotamian church after Sasanid persecution.

apostolic_connection: Disciple of John the Apostle according to Irenaeus.
description: Bishop of Hierapolis (c.110-163); wrote Exposition of Dominical Oracles in 5 volumes (largely lost); key early source for apostolic traditions including accounts of Matthew and Mark's gospels.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 3.36-39; Irenaeus Against Heresies 5.33

description: Named among the Seven in Acts 6.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.

apostolic_connection: Apostle after conversion; worked with Jerusalem leaders; major Gentile missionary.
description: Missionary and letter-writer whose epistles are central to the New Testament and early church expansion.
notes: Martyrdom in Rome is traditional; date approximate.

description: Donatist bishop of Cirta (Numidia); participant in the Donatist controversy of the early 4th century.
citations: Optatus of Mileve, Against the Donatists

description: Bishop of Constantinople (c.337–341; 344–351); Nicene; repeatedly deposed and exiled by Arian emperors; died in exile (possibly strangled) at Cucusus in Armenia.
citations: Socrates, Eccl. Hist. 2.6; Sozomen, Eccl. Hist. 3.4

description: Bishop of Antioch (c.260-272); taught a form of monarchian adoptionism; deposed by the Council of Antioch (268); supported by Queen Zenobia of Palmyra.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 7.27-30; Council of Antioch acts
notes: Council of Nicaea (325) required re-baptism of Paulianists.

description: Bishop of the Nicene congregation at Antioch (361–388); ordained by Lucifer of Cagliari; rejected by the Eastern church as illegitimate; his disputed succession contributed to the Meletian schism.
Bishop of Tyre (c.313–330); Arian sympathizer; close to Eusebius of Caesarea; Eusebius dedicated his Ecclesiastical History to him.

North African noblewoman martyred with Felicitas (7 March 203); her prison diary is preserved in the Passio Perpetuae, one of the earliest writings by a Christian woman; she describes visions and her death in the arena.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.

apostolic_connection: One of the Twelve; leading figure in Jerusalem; associated with Rome in later tradition.
description: Leading apostle in early Christian proclamation; prominent in Acts and early memory.
notes: Death under Nero is traditional; year approximate.

description: Bishop of Alexandria (c.300–311); opponent of Melitius of Lycopolis; wrote canons on readmission of the lapsed; beheaded under Maximin Daia; venerated as the last martyr of the Diocletianic persecution in Egypt.
citations: Eusebius, Eccl. Hist. 8.13; Canons of Peter

apostolic_connection: Recipient of Philemon; associated with house church.
description: Recipient of Paul's letter; associated with a house-church.
False teacher condemned alongside Hymenaeus (2 Tim 2:17); taught that the resurrection had already occurred; excommunicated by Paul.
Apostle mentioned in the Gospels.

apostolic_connection: One of the Seven appointed in Jerusalem (Acts 6).
description: Evangelist in Acts; preached in Samaria and baptized the Ethiopian eunuch.
Bishop of Gortyna (Crete) c.180; wrote a treatise against Marcion; cited by Eusebius.
Bishop of Antioch (319–324); defended Nicene theology before the Council of Nicaea; praised by John Chrysostom in a homily.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Commended in Romans as a servant/deacon and benefactor of many.
Pope (c.142–155); brother of Hermas according to the Muratorian Fragment; during his pontificate Valentinus and Marcion were active in Rome.

Roman senator and author; as governor of Bithynia-Pontus (c.111–113) wrote to Trajan about Christians (Epistle 10.96–97); his letter is the most important pagan source for early Christian worship and persecution.

apostolic_connection: Disciple of John the Apostle; bridge to apostolic generation.
description: Bishop of Smyrna (c.107-155); disciple of the Apostle John; wrote Letter to the Philippians; martyred at an advanced age by fire and sword under Marcus Aurelius.
citations: Irenaeus Against Heresies 3.3; Martyrdom of Polycarp; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 4.14

description: Bishop of Ephesus (c.189); leading figure in the Quartodeciman controversy; wrote to Pope Victor I defending the Asian Passover tradition; claimed descent from a family of seven bishops.
Pope (230–235); exiled to Sardinian mines by Maximinus Thrax; abdicated (first known papal resignation); died in exile; body recovered by Fabian.
First bishop of Lyon; died in prison during the persecution of 177 at the age of 90; his story is told in the Letter of the Churches of Lyon and Vienne.
Bishop of Alexandria (c.98–109) in the Eusebian succession list.

apostolic_connection: Coworker of Paul with Aquila; instructed Apollos.
description: Early Christian teacher and coworker of Paul; often paired with Aquila.
Bishop of Lambaesis; excommunicated at a Numidian council for unspecified crimes; later allied with opponents of Cyprian.
Roman emperor (276–282); generally tolerant of Christians; extended the limits of Rhine-Danube frontier defense; killed by his own troops.
Named among the Seven in Acts 6.
Chief official (protos) of Malta who hosted Paul after the shipwreck (Acts 28:7–8); his father was healed by Paul; later tradition makes him first bishop of Malta.
Named in 2 Timothy among those sending greetings.

Earliest known Christian apologist; presented an apology to Emperor Hadrian c.124; a fragment preserved by Eusebius claims eyewitnesses of Jesus' healings and resurrections were still alive in his day.
Named in Romans among those sending greetings.
Bishop of London; attended the Council of Arles (314); one of the three attested bishops of Roman Britain.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Bishop of Laodicea; martyred during the reign of Verus (c.166); cited by Polycrates of Ephesus in the Quartodeciman controversy.
Deacon of Vienne; martyred in Lyon 177; described in the Letter of the Churches of Lyon and Vienne as refusing to give his name or city under torture, answering only \\\"I am a Christian.\\\"
First bishop of Toulouse; martyred c.250 by being tied to a bull; venerated at the Basilica of Saint-Sernin; one of the earliest bishops of Gaul.
Listed among Paul's companions on part of the third missionary journey.
Bishop of Antioch (c.190–211); wrote against the Gospel of Peter; ordained Palut of Edessa; letters cited by Eusebius.
Roman proconsul of Cyprus (c.45–48); converted to Christianity after Paul's first missionary journey (Acts 13:7–12); the first named Gentile official to convert in Acts.
Catholicos of the Church of the East (c.341–342); martyred with 128 companions during Shapur II's Great Persecution; his martyrdom account (Martyrdom of Šāhdost) survives in Syriac.
citations: Martyrdom of Šāhdost; Chronicle of Seert

description: Sasanian Persian king (309–379); launched the Great Persecution of Christians in Persia (c.340–379), killing thousands including multiple catholicoi; motivated partly by tax disputes and Roman-Persian rivalry after Constantine's conversion.
citations: Martyrdom of Simeon bar Sabbae; Sozomen, Eccl. Hist. 2.9

description: Catholicos of the Church of the East (c.329–344); martyred with five bishops and 100 clergy on Good Friday 344 under Shapur II; first of the great martyr-catholicoi of the Persian persecution.

apostolic_connection: Jerusalem leader and missionary companion of Paul (Acts).
description: Missionary companion of Paul on key journeys recorded in Acts.
Second bishop of Jerusalem; cousin of Jesus according to Hegesippus; martyred under Trajan c.107 at an advanced age; led the Jerusalem community after the flight to Pella.
Named among prophets and teachers in the Antioch church at the launch of Paul's mission.
Righteous man in Luke who blesses the infant Jesus and prophesies.

Samaritan magician who believed and was baptized by Philip (Acts 8); attempted to buy the power of the Spirit from Peter; regarded by the early church as the father of Gnosticism (proto-Gnostic figure); extensive legendary tradition developed.
citations: Acts 8:9–24; Justin Martyr, Apology 1.26; Irenaeus, AH 1.23

description: Tanner in Joppa with whom Peter stayed for many days (Acts 9:43); living by the sea (Acts 10:6); traditional site of his house is a popular pilgrimage destination.
Apostle listed among the Twelve.
Pope (c.115–125) in the Eusebian succession; attested in the Liberian Catalogue.
Pope (257–258); beheaded with four deacons during the Valerian persecution (6 August 258); Cyprian of Carthage was martyred two weeks later; venerated as a major Roman martyr.
Companion of Paul on his final journey from Greece to Jerusalem (Acts 20:4); son of Pyrrhus; may be the same as Sosipater in Romans 16:21.
Named in Romans among Paul's companions sending greetings.

apostolic_connection: Associated with Corinth and co-sender in 1 Corinthians (1 Cor 1:1).
description: Named in Acts in Corinth and in 1 Corinthians as associated with Paul.
Pope (c.166–174); sent aid to the church at Corinth; letter cited by Dionysius of Corinth; known for care of visiting Christians.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named in 1 Corinthians; his household is described as early converts and devoted to service.
Bishop of Corinth (c.260?); correspondent; appears in a letter related to the Novatianist controversy; distinct from Pope Stephen I.

Pope (254–257); strong defender of Roman primacy; disputed with Cyprian and Eastern bishops over the validity of heretical baptism; claimed Petrine authority for his position; martyred under Valerian.

apostolic_connection: One of the Seven appointed in Jerusalem (Acts 6).
description: Deacon and first Christian martyr in Acts.

apostolic_connection: Named among women who supported Jesus (Luke 8:3).
description: Disciple named in Luke among women who supported Jesus' ministry.

Pope (314–335); served throughout Constantine's reign; though not present at Nicaea (325), sent legates; medieval legend credited him with baptizing Constantine and receiving the Donation of Constantine (both fictional).
citations: Eusebius, Life of Constantine; Liber Pontificalis

description: Bishop of Ptolemais (Cyrene) c.410–414; Neoplatonist philosopher who reluctantly accepted episcopal office; author of hymns, letters, and the treatise On Providence; reconciled his Platonic worldview with Christianity.
Named in Philippians among two women urged to agree in the Lord.

apostolic_connection: Disciple in Joppa raised by Peter (Acts 9).
description: Disciple known for charity; raised from death in Acts 9.

Syriac Christian apologist and student of Justin Martyr; wrote Address to the Greeks critiquing paganism; compiled the Diatessaron (gospel harmony) widely used in Syriac Christianity; founded the Encratite movement emphasizing celibacy.
citations: Irenaeus Against Heresies 1.28; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 4.29

description: Pope (c.125–136); only 2nd-century pope whose martyrdom is considered historically certain (attested by Irenaeus); introduced the Gloria in Excelsis and Christmas midnight Mass according to tradition.
citations: Irenaeus, Against Heresies 3.3.3; Eusebius, Eccl. Hist. 4.10

description: Scribe who wrote the Epistle to the Romans and adds his greeting.
First major Latin Christian theologian; invented much Latin theological vocabulary (Trinity, Person, Substance); later became a Montanist; wrote Apology, Against Marcion, Against Praxeas.
notes: Called the Father of Latin Christianity; exact death circumstances unknown.

description: One of the seventy disciples sent to Edessa according to the Doctrine of Addai; identified with the apostle Thaddaeus (Jude); evangelized Osroene; venerated in the Church of the East.
citations: Eusebius, Eccl. Hist. 1.13; Doctrine of Addai

description: Montanist figure of the late 2nd century; wrote a \\\"Catholic Epistle\\\" imitating the apostolic style; mentioned critically by Apollonius in his work against Montanism.
Bishop of Caesarea Maritima (c.230–260); ordained Origen to the presbyterate; supported Origen against Demetrius of Alexandria.
Bishop of Alexandria (c.282–300); baptized Asclepiades (later a palace official under Diocletian); supervised conversion of imperial household members.
Bishop of Antioch (c.169-182); wrote Ad Autolycum, the earliest Christian work to use the word Trinity (triados) in Greek; prominent apologist defending monotheism.
Bishop of Caesarea Maritima under Maximinus Daia; erected a statue of Zeus to persecute Christians; cited by Eusebius as a persecutor; later executed by Licinius.
Apostle remembered in the Gospels; later mission traditions vary.
Bishop of Antioch (c.272–280) in the succession after Paul of Samosata.
Named among the Seven in Acts 6.

apostolic_connection: Coworker and protégé of Paul; recipient in Pastoral Epistles.
description: Paul's close coworker; central figure in Pauline mission and later tradition.

apostolic_connection: Hosted Paul near synagogue in Corinth (Acts 18:7).
description: Worshiper of God in Corinth whose house became a base for Paul's preaching.

apostolic_connection: Coworker of Paul; associated with organizing churches (e.g., Crete).
description: Pauline coworker connected with mediation and church organization.
Character in the Abgar legend; carried correspondence between Abgar of Edessa and Jesus according to Eusebius; the historicity of the story is rejected by modern scholarship.

Roman emperor (98–117); received Pliny the Younger's letter about Christians and instructed that they not be sought out but executed if denounced and unrepentant; his rescript established Roman precedent for Christian persecution.
citations: Pliny, Ep. 10.96–97; Cassius Dio, Roman History 68

apostolic_connection: Travel companion mentioned in Acts and 2 Tim 4:20.
description: Travel companion associated with Ephesus; mentioned in Acts and 2 Timothy.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Courier and coworker mentioned in multiple Pauline letters.
Owner of a lecture hall in Ephesus where Paul taught daily for two years (Acts 19:9); possibly a philosopher or sophist.
Pope (222–230); tradition holds he converted many including Valerian and Tiburtius; the martyrdom account is legendary; his pontificate overlapped with Origen's period of influence.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Bishop of Mursa (c.343); leading Arian/homoian bishop alongside Ursacius; had great influence over Constantius II; signed multiple anti-Nicene formulas.
citations: Hilary of Poitiers, Against Valens and Ursacius

description: Egyptian-born gnostic teacher who moved to Rome c.136; founded the most influential gnostic school; reportedly almost elected bishop of Rome; authored the Gospel of Truth (possibly).
citations: Irenaeus Against Heresies 1.1-9; Tertullian Against the Valentinians

description: Bishop of Saragossa (Caesaraugusta) in the early 4th century; associated with Vincent of Saragossa who served as his deacon; attended the Council of Elvira (c.306).
Wealthy young Christian of Lyon; martyred 177; volunteered to defend the accused Christians before the governor; described in the Letter of the Churches of Lyon as full of the Holy Spirit.
Bishop of Rome (189-199); first Latin-speaking pope; attempted to excommunicate Asian churches over the Quartodeciman Easter controversy; first to assert strong Roman episcopal authority.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 5.23-25; Irenaeus intervening letter

name_alt: Vincent of Saragossa; Vincent the Deacon
description: Deacon of Saragossa; martyred under Dacian (c.22 January 304) in Valencia; his torture and death described in detail by Prudentius; patron saint of Saragossa, Lisbon, and Valencia.
Martyr of Milan; traditionally identified as a soldier martyred under Nero or Marcus Aurelius; venerated at the Basilica of Santi Vitale e Agricola in Bologna; Acts are late and unreliable.
Bishop of Edessa (c.180); in the Edessan episcopal succession; active during the period of Bardesanes (Bardaisan).
Catholicos of Armenia (c.333–341); son of Gregory the Illuminator; succeeded his brother Aristakes; expanded Armenian Christianity.
Bishop of Jerusalem (c.273–283) in the Jerusalem episcopal succession list preserved by Eusebius.
Tax collector in Luke who responds to Jesus and practices restitution.
Bishop of Antioch (c.240–244) in the Antiochene succession; minimal information survives.
Pope (199–217); led the Roman church through the Monarchian controversy; his deacon Callixtus dominated church administration; condemned Sabellius; criticized by Hippolytus as theologically weak.

Late 1st-century Gnostic teacher active in Asia Minor, especially Ephesus; taught that the material world was made by an inferior demiurge angel; distinguished between the human Jesus and the heavenly Christ who descended at his baptism and departed before the crucifixion; combined elements of Jewish law-observance with proto-Gnostic cosmology; identified by Irenaeus (via Polycarp) as the direct adversary of the Apostle John at Ephesus—John reportedly fled a bathhouse when he discovered Cerinthus inside.
citations: Irenaeus, Against Heresies 1.26.1; 3.3.4; Eusebius, Eccl. Hist. 3.28.1–2

description: Latin Christian apologist and rhetorician, known as the "Christian Cicero" for his classically polished prose; taught rhetoric in Nicomedia at the invitation of Diocletian before converting to Christianity; wrote Divinae Institutiones (7 books; the first systematic Latin apology for Christianity, c.304–313), De Opificio Dei (on human creation), and De Mortibus Persecutorum (a vivid account of the divine punishment of emperors who persecuted Christians, including an account of Constantine's vision); appointed c.317 by Constantine as tutor to his son Crispus in Gaul.
citations: Jerome, Vir. Ill. 80; Eusebius, Vita Constantini 1.12

description: Iranian prophet born April 14, 216 in southern Babylonia; founded Manichaeism, a universalist dualistic religion blending Zoroastrian, Buddhist, and Christian elements around the cosmic myth of the war of Light and Darkness; began his public mission c.240 under Shapur I's patronage, traveling to India and throughout the Persian Empire; composed seven foundational scriptures (the only ancient religious founder to produce a personal canonical corpus); arrested c.274 under Bahram I at the instigation of the Zoroastrian high priest Kartir; died in chains awaiting execution, c.274/277. Manichaeism spread westward, competing directly with Christianity in the Roman Empire.
citations: Cologne Mani Codex; Kephalaia; al-Biruni, Chronology of Ancient Nations; Encyclopaedia Iranica

description: Bishop of Olympus in Lycia (Asia Minor), and possibly later of Patara or Tyre; the most distinguished early theological opponent of Origen; wrote the Symposium or Banquet of the Ten Virgins (a Platonic-style dialogue on virginity as the supreme Christian virtue, c.290), On the Resurrection (a direct refutation of Origen's allegorical interpretation of resurrection), and On Free Will (against Gnostic fatalism); also wrote against porphyry; martyred at Chalcis in Greece c.311 at the close of the Diocletianic persecution.
citations: Jerome, Vir. Ill. 83; Epiphanius, Panarion 64; Socrates, Eccl. Hist. 6.13

description: Early modalist (Patripassian) theologian from Smyrna, fl. c.200–210; taught that God the Father himself was born as Jesus Christ, suffered, and died on the cross—a position his opponents called Patripassianism (the Father suffers); excommunicated by a council of Smyrnaean presbyters after two examinations; his disciple Epigonus carried the teaching to Rome where it was further developed by Cleomenes; directly opposed by Hippolytus of Rome in his treatise Against Noetus.
citations: Hippolytus, Against Noetus 1; Hippolytus, Refutatio Omnium Haeresium 9.10

description: Modalist monarchian teacher from Asia Minor (possibly Phrygia), fl. c.190–210; had endured imprisonment for the faith (a "confessor") which gave him prestige; traveled to Rome where he reportedly suppressed nascent Montanism and promoted Patripassian modalism; later traveled to Carthage; opposed by Tertullian in Adversus Praxeam (c.213), which preserves the only significant evidence for his teaching; reportedly composed a written recantation at Tertullian's insistence, though its content is unknown; the name "Praxeas" may be a polemical pseudonym coined by Tertullian meaning roughly "busybody."
citations: Tertullian, Adversus Praxeam 1–2; Jerome, De Vir. Ill. 53 (implicit)

description: Most prominent teacher of modalistic monarchianism, active in Rome c.215–220; likely of Libyan or Egyptian origin; taught that the one undivided God (the Monad) reveals himself successively as Father (in creation and law), Son (in incarnation), and Holy Spirit (in sanctification and prophecy)—the divine Monad "expands" into each mode without real internal distinction; excommunicated by Callixtus I c.220; later also condemned by Dionysius of Alexandria (c.260) and Dionysius of Rome (c.262), whose exchange on Sabellianism is a key pre-Nicene Trinitarian document.
citations: Hippolytus, Refutatio 9.12; Callixtus I decree in Hippolytus; Athanasius, De sententia Dionysii 5

description: Legendary female apostle and disciple of Paul from Iconium; central figure of the Acts of Paul and Thecla (c.160–200 AD), a widely circulated narrative in which she hears Paul preach virginity in Iconium, breaks her betrothal, endures martyrdom attempts at Iconium and Antioch (miraculously surviving both), baptizes herself in a pool of seals in the arena, and becomes an itinerant preacher; venerated as "equal to the Apostles" and the first female martyr in Eastern tradition; invoked in early debates about women baptizing and teaching (Tertullian explicitly criticized the Acts of Paul and Thecla in De Baptismo 17 as a forged text used to justify women baptizing).
citations: Acts of Paul and Thecla (c.160–200); Tertullian, De Baptismo 17; Eusebius, Eccl. Hist. 3.29 (implicit)

description: Bishop of Poetovio (modern Ptuj, Slovenia); the earliest known Latin biblical commentator; wrote commentaries on Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Isaiah, Ezekiel, Habakkuk, Ecclesiastes, Song of Songs, Revelation, and Matthew — the majority are lost; his Commentary on the Apocalypse (Revelation) survives, partly in a revised form by Jerome who softened its strong millenarianism; also wrote On the Creation of the World; his biblical interpretation was strongly millenarian; martyred under Diocletian c.303/304.
citations: Jerome, Vir. Ill. 74; Jerome, Commentary on the Apocalypse (revised edition)

description: Latin Christian apologist; author of the Octavius, a Ciceronian-style dialogue between the pagan Caecilius and the Christian Octavius set at the seaside resort of Ostia; one of the earliest Latin Christian literary works; argues for monotheism and the rationality of Christianity without directly citing Scripture; closely related to Tertullian's Apologeticus (the direction of dependence is debated).
citations: Jerome, Vir. Ill. 58; Lactantius, Div. Inst. 1.11; 5.1

description: First Christian chronographer; wrote the Chronographiae (five books, c.221), a universal history from Creation to 221 AD that established the framework for all subsequent Christian chronography; also wrote the Kestoi (Cesti), a miscellany of secular knowledge on medicine, agriculture, and military tactics; corresponded with Origen on the authenticity of the story of Susanna in Daniel; helped design the library at the Pantheon in Rome for Emperor Alexander Severus; founded the colony of Emmaus-Nicopolis.
citations: Eusebius, Eccl. Hist. 6.31; Jerome, Vir. Ill. 63; Syncellus, Chronographia
